description: Provides exploration benefits, map information, and navigation assistance

tieredBenefits.description: Basic exploration bonuses, reveals nearby points of interest
Wider discovery radius, marks resource locations and enemy camps
Marks dungeons/spawners, 1.5x wisplight radius in Mistlands
Maximum discovery, all dungeons marked, 2x wisplight radius

randomTalk: Running away gets a bad reputation. I prefer to think of it as scouting in the opposite direction.
I classify threats on a scale from 'concerning' to 'tell my mother I loved her.' Most of the Plains is a solid 'tell my mother.'
I spent three days in a tree once, waiting for a troll to leave. The troll spent three days under the tree, waiting for me to come down. On day four he picked up the tree and I went home.
Everyone thinks the dark is scary. The dark is FINE. It's what's UNDER the dark that gets you.
I've developed a classification system for bushes. 'Safe bush.' 'Probably safe bush.' 'Bush with eyes.' You learn to spot the difference.
The trick to surviving out there is simple: don't be the slowest person in the group. I keep a mental ranking. Don't ask where you are on it.
People think scouting is about what you find. It's not. Scouting is about what doesn't find YOU.
My reports are very thorough. Page one: terrain. Page two: weather. Page three: reasons not to go there. Page three is usually the longest.
A greydwarf alone is nothing. Two greydwarfs are a nuisance. Forty greydwarfs are a Tuesday. I have SEEN some Tuesdays.
I once hid so well I forgot where I was. I missed dinner.
The guard says 'protect the village.' The warrior says 'fight the enemy.' The scout says 'have you SEEN the enemy? Because I have. And I have CONCERNS.'
I can move through a forest without making a sound. The forest, however, makes PLENTY of sounds. Most of them are threats. Some of them are specific.
Bravery is just ignorance with better publicity. Cowardice requires genuine intelligence about what's out there. I am VERY intelligent.
People romanticize the lone wolf. You know what happens to lone wolves? Other wolves eat them. I'm more of a lone hiding-behind-a-rock. Less romantic. More alive.
randomGreets: Shh! ...Oh, it's just you. Sorry, I've been on edge. On edge of what, you ask? EVERYTHING.
Back from patrol! I have a detailed report. Summary: don't go out there. Extended version: REALLY don't go out there.
Ah! There you are. I knew you were coming. I know everything that moves within half a league. Except squirrels. Squirrels are CHAOS.
Good news! I'm alive. That IS the good news. I'll let you know when I have other kinds.
You won't BELIEVE what I've seen. Well, you might. You've got that look. The look of someone who's going to go see for themselves no matter WHAT I say.
Don't mind the twitching. It goes away after a few minutes indoors. Usually.
randomGoodbye: Right. Off into the wilderness. If I'm not back in three days, avenge me. Or don't. The wilderness won't care either way.
Time to disappear. And I mean that literally, not the 'oh he's dead' way. Although keep both options open.
I'll be the one in the bushes. If you see someone ELSE in the bushes, that's not me, and you should run.
Stay safe! Specifically, stay inside. With the doors locked. And something heavy against the doors.
Off to scout! Remember: if you hear screaming in the distance, it's probably not me. Probably.
